perturbation.original_text: Fur ther, in the event of litigation relating to this Agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover its reasonable attorney’s fees and expenses.
perturbation.changed_text: Further, in the event of litigation relating to this Agreement, each party shall bear its own attorney’s fees and expenses, regardless of the outcome.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a legal contradiction by removing the standard provision for the prevailing party to recover attorney's fees. Many jurisdictions, including those in Canada, have rules regarding cost allocation in litigation which can be impacted by such clauses.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Rules of Court in Newfoundland and Labrador, Canada, specifically Rule 73 regarding costs.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 73 outlines the general principles and specific rules for awarding costs in legal proceedings in Newfoundland and Labrador. By stating that each party bears their own costs regardless of outcome, the agreement contradicts the court's ability to award costs to the prevailing party, which is a standard practice governed by the Rules of Court.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 73. Magistrate Judges: Trial by Consent; Appeal | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 73. Magistrate Judges: Trial by Consent; Appeal (a) Trial by Consent. When authorized under 28 U.S.C. §636(c) , a magistrate judge may, if all parties consent, conduct a civil action or proceeding, including a jury or nonjury trial. A record must be made in accordance with 28 U.S.C. §636(c)(5) . (b) Consent Procedure. (1) In General. When a magistrate judge has been designated to conduct civil actions or proceedings, the clerk must give the parties written notice of their opportunity to consent under 28 U.S.C. §636(c) . To signify their consent, the parties must jointly or separately file a statement consenting to the referral. A district judge or magistrate judge may be informed of a party's response to the clerk's notice only if all parties have consented to the referral. (2) Reminding the Parties About Consenting. A district judge, magistrate judge, or other court official may remind the parties of the magistrate judge's availability, but must also advise them that they are free to withhold consent without adverse substantive consequences. (3) Vacating a Referral. On its own for good cause—or when a party shows extraordinary circumstances—the district judge may vacate a referral to a magistrate judge under this rule. (c) Appealing a Judgment. In accordance with 28 U.S.C. §636(c)(3) , an appeal from a judgment entered at a magistrate judge's direction may be taken to the court of appeals as would any other appeal from a district-court judgment. Notes (As added Apr. 28, 1983, eff. Aug. 1, 1983; amended Mar. 2, 1987, eff. Aug. 1, 1987; Apr. 22, 1993, eff. Dec. 1, 1993; Apr. 11, 1997, eff. Dec. 1, 1997; Apr. 30, 2007, eff. Dec. 1, 2007.) Notes of Advisory Committee on Rules—1983 Subdivision (a) . This subdivision implements the broad authority of the 1979 amendments to the Magistrates Act, 2
perturbation.justification: The original contract allows the prevailing party in litigation to recover attorney's fees and expenses. The changed text explicitly states that each party will bear its own attorney's fees, regardless of the outcome. The Law Explanation states that Rule 73 outlines rules for awarding costs in legal proceedings. By stating that each party bears their own costs, the agreement contradicts the court's ability to award costs to the prevailing party. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references Rule 73, although it pertains to magistrate judges and doesn't directly address the awarding of attorney's fees, it hints at court's ability to manage costs. The omission of the prevailing party's entitlement to attorney's fees and the explicit statement that each party bears their own costs, directly contradicts the standard practice, therefore, a legal contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, the parties’ duty to hold in confidence Confidential Information that was disclosed during term shall remain in effect indefinitely.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, the parties’ duty to hold in confidence Confidential Information that was disclosed during term shall cease after the agreement expires.
perturbation.explanation: This change significantly alters the confidentiality obligations, which can contradict trade secret laws. By limiting the duty to hold information confidential to the term of the agreement, information that might otherwise be protected as a trade secret could be disclosed after the agreement expires, violating relevant trade secret laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Canadian law, specifically the common law regarding trade secrets and confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: Canadian common law recognizes and protects trade secrets. Setting a time limit for confidentiality, even for trade secrets, will make it harder for the disclosing party to seek remedies, especially if the recipient begins using or disclosing the trade secret after the expiration of the agreement but the information still qualifies as a trade secret. The change contradicts the indefinite protection often afforded to trade secrets under common law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Regulation On Microcredit Companies And Microcredit Operators - Financial Services - Angola ARTICLE 2 May 2023 Regulation On Microcredit Companies And Microcredit Operators P PLMJ More Contributor PLMJ is a law firm based in Portugal that combines a full service with bespoke legal craftsmanship. For more than 50 years, the firm has taken an innovative and creative approach to produced tailor-made solutions to effectively defend the interests of its clients. The firm supports its clients in all areas of the law, often with multidisciplinary teams, and always acting as a business partner in the most strategic decision-making processes. Explore Firm Details The Regulation on Microcredit Companies and Microcredit Operators was approved by Presidential Decree 89/23 of 31 March. It repeals Presidential Decree 28/11 of 2 February and entered into force on 1 April. Angola Finance and Banking Gonçalo Dos Reis Martins, Renata Valenti, and Joana Marques Dos Reis Your Author LinkedIn Connections To print this article, all you need is to be registered or login on Mondaq.com. The Regulation on Microcredit Companies and Microcredit Operators was approved by Presidential Decree 89/23 of 31 March. It repeals Presidential Decree 28/11 of 2 February and entered into force on 1 April. The Regulation lays down the rules for the operation of microcredit institutions and applies to institutions engaged in microcredit activities, namely: microcredit companies, defined as non-bank financial institutions engaged in microcredit activities and authorised to grant low and medium value loans to small and medium-sized enterprises; and micro-credit operators, defined as non-financial commercial enterprises whose purpose includes the granting of micro-credit on a non-exclusive basis, such as NGOs, associations and foundations. For the purposes of the Regulation, "microcredit" means credit granted to micro and small enterprises, which may be natural persons or legal entities, on the basis of joint or individual liability, to carry out an economic activity, the amount of which is determined by the Banco Nacional de Angola ("BNA"). Operating as a microcredit provider is subject to authorisation by the BNA, in accordance with the provisions of the Law on the General Framework of Financial Institutions and complementary regulations. The Regulation defines the positive and negative elements that constitute the basic own funds of microcredit institutions in order to cover the risks or losses they may incur. It also establishes the possibility of using the following means of financing in the event that the resources of microcredit institutions are insufficient to meet their obligations: (i) reserves from the revaluation of fixed assets; (ii) other positive revaluation reserves; (iii) subordinated loans with a maturity of more than 5 years, the terms of which are approved by the BNA, which may constitute up to 50% of the basic own funds; and (iv) hybrid capital and debt instruments. The Regulation establishes the principle of free negotiation of interest rates, with the exception of the possibility for the BNA to set and publish, from time to time, maximum limits on the interest rate that credit institutions may charge in situations that may constitute usury. As provided in the previous legislation, microcredit institutions may not receive deposits, except for (i) loan payments made by borrowers before the due date by way of amortisation and (ii) the possible transfer of monetary values by the borrower as a guarantee for the loan to be granted. The Regulation also provides that microcredit companies may invest their available resources in low-risk financial operations in a diversified manner. The content of this article is intended to provide a general guide to the subject matter. Specialist advice should be sought about your specific circumstances. perturbation.justification: The original text ensures indefinite confidentiality, whereas the changed text limits it to the agreement's term. The Law Explanation states that setting a time limit for confidentiality will make it harder for the disclosing party to seek remedies, especially if the recipient begins using or disclosing the trade secret after the expiration of the agreement. Since the Changed Text explicitly puts a time limit on the confidential information, it undermines the protection of trade secrets by allowing them to be disclosed after the agreement expires.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Pa rty shall immediately return and redeliver to the other all tangible material embodying the Confidential Information p rovided hereunder and all notes, summaries, memoranda, drawin gs, manuals, records, excerpts or derivative information deriving there from and all other docu ments or materials (“Notes”) (and all copies of any of the foregoing, including “cop ies” that have been con verted to computerized media in the form of image, da ta or word processing files either manua lly or by image capture) based on or including any Confidential Information, in whatever form of storage or retrieval, upon the earlier of (i) the completion or termination of the dealings between the parties contemplated hereunder; (ii) the termination of this Agreement; or (iii) at such time as the Disclosing Party may so request; provided ho wever that the Receiving Pa rty may retain such of its documents as is necessary to enable it to comply with its document retention policies.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Pa rty shall return and redeliver to the other all tangible material embodying the Confidential Information p rovided hereunder. The Receiving Pa rty may retain such of its documents as is necessary to enable it to comply with its document retention policies.
perturbation.explanation: This omits a significant portion of the return obligations. The deletion removes the explicit requirement to return notes, summaries, memoranda, drawings, manuals, records, excerpts, derivative information and other materials (and copies thereof) based on Confidential Information. This could violate legal obligations related to the protection of trade secrets and confidential information, particularly regarding the destruction or return of derivative works.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Canadian common law regarding trade secrets and confidential information, particularly the requirement to protect and not misuse confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: By allowing the receiving party to retain summaries, notes and derivative works containing confidential information even after the agreement terminates, this change weakens the protections afforded to the disclosing party under Canadian trade secret law. While document retention policies are considered, the overbroad allowance to retain materials directly derived from the confidential information conflicts with the duty to protect and return or destroy confidential materials.

Trade secrets are intellectual property (IP) rights on confidential information which may be sold or licensed. What qualifies as a trade secret? In general, to qualify as a trade secret, the information must be: commercially valuable because it is secret, be known only to a limited group of persons , and be subject to reasonable steps taken by the rightful holder of the information to keep it secret, including the use of confidentiality agreements for business partners and employees. The unauthorized acquisition, use or disclosure of such secret information in a manner contrary to honest commercial practices by others is regarded as an unfair practice and a violation of the trade secret protection. perturbation.justification: The changed text omits the requirement to return 'notes, summaries, memoranda, drawings, manuals, records, excerpts or derivative information' derived from confidential information, allowing the receiving party to retain these materials even after the agreement terminates. This directly contradicts the obligation to protect trade secrets and confidential information, particularly concerning the return or destruction of derivative works, as highlighted in the perturbation and law explanations. The WIPO snippet defines a trade secret as requiring 'reasonable steps taken by the rightful holder of the information to keep it secret, including the use of confidentiality agreements' which is undermined by allowing derivative materials to be retained.

perturbation.original_text: The validity, construction and performance of this Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of Newfoundland, Canada applicable to contracts made and to be wholly performed within such state, without giving effect to any conflict of laws provisions thereof. The Federal and state courts located in Newfoundland, Canada , shall have sole and exc lusive jurisdiction over any disputes arising under the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The validity, construction and performance of this Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of Newfoundland, Canada. The parties agree to resolve any disputes through binding arbitration in a location of the Receiving Party's choosing.
perturbation.explanation: This replaces the specified court jurisdiction with mandatory binding arbitration. This is problematic because it removes a consumer's right to access the court system, which is a right granted under Provincial law, and dictates that the location of the arbitration will be chosen by the receiving party, which is heavily skewed in their favor. It is generally accepted that this clause introduces bias and is prejudicial to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.law_explanation: By forcing binding arbitration and granting the Receiving Party the choice of location, the changed text potentially undermines the principles of fairness and impartiality that are intended to be safeguarded by the Arbitration Act. While arbitration is generally permissible, a clause that fundamentally biases the process toward one party could be challenged under the Act and common law principles of unconscionability and unequal bargaining power.

perturbation.justification: The original text specifies court jurisdiction in Newfoundland, Canada. The changed text replaces this with mandatory binding arbitration, with the Receiving Party choosing the location. The Perturbation Explanation states this removes a consumer's right to access the court system and introduces bias. The Law Explanation suggests this undermines fairness principles, and could be challenged under the Arbitration Act due to unconscionability and unequal bargaining power. Although the scraped snippets are error pages, the absence of explicit allowance for such a drastic change, combined with the explanations, indicates a contradiction. The change actively removes a legal avenue and introduces potential bias, rather than simply weakening or making it ambiguous.
